description: This figure displays a finite element model of an automotive body-in-white structure, rendered as a transparent wireframe mesh showing the vehicle chassis architecture with two specific structural components highlighted in solid red. Based on the paper's context regarding crashworthiness simulations of low-density steels, these highlighted regions represent critical structural members—likely the A-pillars or front side members (FSM)—that were subjected to dynamic compression analysis using LS-DYNA finite element software under EuroNCAP 40% offset front crash conditions. The visualization supports the evaluation of advanced high-aluminum low-density steel grades (specifically 7.4Al-8.2Mn-0.2C austenite-ferrite and 9.3Al-25.8Mn-0.96C fully austenitic compositions) as lightweight replacements for conventional DP600 dual-phase steel in automotive structural applications. The simulation results demonstrate that these low-density steel components can achieve substantial weight reductions of 21% to 33% while maintaining required crash performance standards, illustrating the potential of complex metallurgical design for automotive lightweighting strategies.
